title: Atrous Convolutions
concept: Atrous (dilated) convolutions expand a filter's receptive field without losing spatial resolution or increasing parameter counts.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main speed-accuracy trade-off in Mask R-CNN?

options.text: Heavy backbones like ResNet provide higher accuracy but slower inference speeds
Light backbones like MobileNet have slower training but faster inference
Mask R-CNN is fast but only works on low-resolution photos

exercises.explain: Correct! Heavy backbones improve masks but increase latency.

cards.title: The resolution trap
cards.body: To see large objects, networks use pooling layers to shrink features, expanding the 'receptive field' (how much of the image a filter sees). But downsampling destroys spatial detail! We want a filter that can see a large area *without* shrinking the image. How do we expand our view without losing resolution?

cards.title: Atrous Convolution
cards.body: An Atrous Convolution (also called dilated convolution) inserts spaces ('holes') inside a convolutional kernel. By spacing out the weights, the filter covers a much larger area without adding any parameters or downsampling the features.

cards.title: Spreading your fingers
cards.body: Imagine placing your hand on a table with your fingers pressed together — you cover a small area. Now, spread your fingers wide. Your hand covers a much larger area, but you still have the same five fingers. That is a dilated convolution.

cards.title: Dilation rate
cards.body: A standard 3x3 filter has a dilation rate of $r=1$ and sees a 3x3 area. By setting the dilation rate to $r=2$, we insert spaces between the weights, and the filter now spans a 5x5 area while still using only 9 parameters.

cards.title: No extra parameters
cards.body: You might think a filter covering a 5x5 area has 25 parameters. In an atrous convolution with $r=2$, it still has exactly 9 weights! The spaces between are just filled with zeros, meaning it has the same computational weight as a 3x3 conv.

cards.title: Standard vs. Atrous Convolution
cards.body: Comparing filter coverage at same parameter count:

left.text:
3x3 kernel, tight grid
Covers 3x3 pixels
Needs pooling to see larger zones

right.text:
3x3 kernel, spaced out
Covers 5x5 pixels (spaces between weights)
Expands field without pooling

exercises.prompt: What is the primary benefit of atrous convolutions in image segmentation?

options.text: They expand the receptive field without downsampling or adding parameters
They speed up training by deleting weights
They combine bounding boxes with segmentation masks

exercises.explain: Correct! Dilated convolutions expand receptive field while preserving resolution.

exercises.prompt: True or False: An atrous convolution with a dilation rate of 2 uses more weight parameters than a standard convolution.
exercises.explain: False. It uses the same number of weights; the spaces between are filled with zeros.

exercises.sentence: Atrous convolutions insert spaces, or '___', inside the convolutional kernel.
exercises.explain: The word 'atrous' comes from French 'à trous', meaning 'with holes'.

exercises.prompt: What happens to the receptive field as the dilation rate increases?
exercises.explain: Increasing the dilation rate stretches the filter, expanding the receptive field.

exercises.prompt: You are segmenting roads in satellite images. Standard convolutions require deep pooling to see the whole road, resulting in blocky borders. How do atrous convolutions solve this?

options.text: They allow the filters to span a wide area to capture the road's context while maintaining high-resolution feature maps for sharp borders.
They automatically convert the satellite image to a vector shape.
They run only on the background sky pixels, ignoring the road.
They use fewer channels in the encoder, speeding up execution.

exercises.explain: Perfect! Atrous convolutions let the model see the big picture (context) and the small details (resolution) simultaneously, without trade-offs. Next: how do we pool features at multiple scales?
